coredata.dc:title: One-step microwave method realizes two-step strategy to enhance the photocatalytic performance of fly ash: Enhance the carrier transfer efficiency and inhibit the carrier recombination
coredata.dc:creator: He, S.
Luo, L.
Jiang, T.
Wang, G.
Liu, Y.
Fan, T.
Zhu, X.
Wei, X.
Wang, F.
Jing, J.
coredata.dc:description: To address the negative impacts of fly ash (FA), its potential applications in various fields, including photocatalysis, should be investigated. In this study, a photocatalyst (P–C-dots@FA/P–C) with phosphorized carbon-dots (C-dots) and carbon layer on FA was synthesized by a one-step microwave method. To obtain the optimal catalytic activity, the synthesis conditions of different phosphoric acid contents and microwave times were tested. The addition of 18% phosphoric acid yielded the highest catalytic activity under a microwave of 700 W for 4 min. In addition, the photocatalytic activity of P–C-dots@FA/P–C for Rhodamine B was 238 times higher than that of FA alone. Furthermore, the doping of phosphorus resulted in the formation of C-dots on the FA surface, thereby obtaining the P–C-dots@FA material with an enhanced photocatalytic activity of P–C. This material enhanced the transfer of photogenerated carriers, was further carbonized to form a carbon layer, and weakened the recombination of photogenerated carriers. The stability experiment of the material proves that the material has good reproducibility, stability, and recyclability.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Photocatalysis
Microwave synthesis
Rhodamine B
Visible light
Band regulation

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Preparation of P–C-dots@FA/P–C, P–C-dots@FA, and P–C

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work is financially supported by Sichuan Province Science and Technology Support Program (No. 2021YFN0130).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: SEM of (a) FA, (b) 18P–C, (c) P–C-dots@FA, and (d) P–C-dots@FA/18P–C, (e) EDS of P–C-dots@FA/18P–C. C-dots, carbon-dots; EDS, energy-dispersive X-ray spectroscopy; FA, fly ash; SEM, scanning electron microscopy.

(a), (b) TEM of P–C-dots@FA/18P–C and (c) P–C-dots@FA, (d) EDS of P–C-dots@FA/18P–C. C-dots, carbon-dots; EDS, energy-dispersive X-ray spectroscopy; FA, fly ash; TEM, transmission electron microscopy.
(a) XRD and (b) FTIR spectra of FA, P–C-dots@FA, P–C-dots@FA/18P–C, and 18P–C. C-dots, carbon-dots; FA, fly ash; FTIR, Fourier transform infrared; XRD, X-ray diffraction.

(a), (b) XPS spectra of FA, 18P–C, P–C-dots@FA/18P–C, and P–C-dots@FA, (c) Al XPS of FA, P–C-dots@FA, P–C-dots@FA/18P–C, 18P–C. C-dots, carbon-dots; FA, fly ash; XPS, X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy.
(a) Transient PC response, (b) EIS spectra, (c) PL emission spectra under the excitation of 375 nm. EIS, electrochemical impedance spectroscopy; PC, photocurrent; PL, photoluminescence.

(a) UV–vis absorption spectra of the as-prepared catalysts and (b) (ahv)2 vs. hv curves, The four materials exhibited their visible light absorption capacity. First, the carbon (P–C-dots@FA) deposited on the FA surface reduced the band gap of FA and decreased the band gap of P–C-dots@FA/18P–C. The narrow band gap resulted in the red shift of the light absorption and improved the utilization of light with long wavelengths. This resulted in the easier light excitation of the electrons in the VB to the CB, which is conducive to the generation of photogenerated carriers. . (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the Web version of this article.) CB, conduction band; C-dots, carbon-dots; FA, fly ash; UV–vis, ultraviolet–visible; VB, valence band.
Mott–Schottky plots for (a) FA, (b) 18P–C, (c) P–C-dots@FA, and (d) P–C-dots@FA/18P–C. C-dots, carbon-dots; FA, fly ash.
Photocatalytic mechanism of FA, 18P–C, P–C-dots@FA, and P–C-dots@FA/18P–C. C-dots, carbon-dots; FA, fly ash.

(a) Adsorption capacity of the as-prepared photocatalysts for Rh B after achieving absorption–desorption equilibrium (90 min in a dark environment), (b) photocatalytic ability of the catalysts based on the photocatalytic degradation of Rh B under visible light, (c) corresponding kinetic simulation curve. Rh B, rhodamine B.

(a) Photocatalytic degradation rates of 50 prepared materials of composite P–C-dot@FA/18P–C (5 experimental groups, 10 parallel samples each), (b) photodegradation cycle of composite P–C-dot@FA/18P–C under visible light irradiation (n = 5). C-dot, carbon-dot; FA, fly ash.

Relationship between adsorption rate and photocatalytic degradation rate of 50 prepared materials of composite P–C-dot@FA/18P–C (5 experimental groups, 10 parallel samples each). C-dot, carbon-dot; FA, fly ash.

DMPO EPR spectra with 5% P–C-dots@FA/18P–C in the (a) methanol dispersion for DMPO-O2 - and (b) aqueous dispersion for DMPO-∙OH. C-dot, carbon-dots; DMPO, 5,5-dimethyl-1-pyrroline N-oxide; EPR, electron paramagnetic resonance; FA, fly ash.

head.ce:title: One-step microwave method realizes two-step strategy to enhance the photocatalytic performance of fly ash: Enhance the carrier transfer efficiency and inhibit the carrier recombination

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Materials and Environmental Engineering, Chengdu Technological University, Chengdu 611730, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Materials and Environmental Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Materials and Environmental Engineering, Chengdu Technological University, Chengdu 611730, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Humanities and Design, Chengdu Technological University, Chengdu 611730, China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Humanities and Design, Chengdu Technological University, Chengdu 611730, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Intelligent Manufacturing, Chengdu Technological University, Chengdu 611730, China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Intelligent Manufacturing, Chengdu Technological University, Chengdu 611730, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: To address the negative impacts of fly ash (FA), its potential applications in various fields, including photocatalysis, should be investigated. In this study, a photocatalyst (P–C-dots@FA/P–C) with phosphorized carbon-dots (C-dots) and carbon layer on FA was synthesized by a one-step microwave method. To obtain the optimal catalytic activity, the synthesis conditions of different phosphoric acid contents and microwave times were tested. The addition of 18% phosphoric acid yielded the highest catalytic activity under a microwave of 700 W for 4 min. In addition, the photocatalytic activity of P–C-dots@FA/P–C for Rhodamine B was 238 times higher than that of FA alone. Furthermore, the doping of phosphorus resulted in the formation of C-dots on the FA surface, thereby obtaining the P–C-dots@FA material with an enhanced photocatalytic activity of P–C. This material enhanced the transfer of photogenerated carriers, was further carbonized to form a carbon layer, and weakened the recombination of photogenerated carriers. The stability experiment of the material proves that the material has good reproducibility, stability, and recyclability.

ce:section.ce:para: Fly ash (FA) is a powdery solid waste produced by coal combustion in coal-fired power plants. Without proper handling, FA can pose serious and lasting environmental problems. In particular, the global annual FA production is approximately 600–800 million tons [1] with 10% ascribed to the United States and Europe, and 18% to India and China [2,3]. Currently, FA is primarily used in construction, road construction, waste solidification, mining, soil modification, and other industries [4]. Nevertheless, the amount of FA reutilization (30%) is lower than the total amount of generated FA [1,5]. Thus, studies focused on the effective reutilization of FA are imperative [4].

FA is composed of SiO2, Al2O3, Fe2O3, and small amounts of trace elements (Cr, Ba, Ni, Pb, Sr, and Zn) [6]. Moreover, it has a spherical structure, good thermal insulation performance, and strong high-temperature resistance [7]. These semiconductor materials and metal oxides can provide effective active sites for photocatalytic reactions [8]. Moreover, FA with low density can float in water, which suggests potential application for solar energy harvesting [9,10]. Therefore, multiple attempts have been made on the exploration of FA in photocatalysis, including through carrier materials [11,12], heterojunctions [13,14], cocatalysts [15], and self-modification [16]. Among them, self-modification can achieve the highest utilization rate. Nonetheless, the excessively fast carrier recombination speed during modification and ultraviolet light absorption range limits the photocatalytic activity of FA. In addition, the metal elements in the materials can easily leach out, resulting in secondary pollution and by which the catalyst loses its active components [17]. Thus, the photocatalytic performance and stability of pulverized FA should be improved.

As a zero-dimensional carbon, carbon-dots (C-dots) have been successfully applied in combination with semiconductor materials, such as tungsten trioxide, titanium dioxide, silver phosphate, and iron oxide, to improve their photocatalytic capability [18–22]. In particular, C-dots have advantages in controlling the bandgap, promoting carrier separation, and controlling charge transfer. Gao et al. [23] studied the interaction between g-C3N4 and C-dots through density functional theory. The introduction of C-dots significantly reduced the bandgap, advanced the separation of photoinduced carriers, and reinforced visible light response. Ma et al. [24] characterized the interfacial electronic coupling of C-dots/g-C3N4 composites. The bandgap of C-dots/g-C3N4 could be controlled by changing the transverse size of the C-dots, whereby an ideal 1.92 eV solar light acquisition bandgap was obtained. Feng et al. [25] found that the surface functional groups of C-dots are useful in determining the charge transfer direction and active sites for water reduction oxidation. Li et al. [26] found that C-dots with phosphorus exhibited enhanced fluorescence and improved light quantum yields, compared with separate C-dots. Further, Xiong et al. [27] introduced phosphorized C-dots into g-C3N4, thereby improving the photoelectric separation efficiency of g-C3N4 with a low separation rate of the photogenerated carriers. These results confirmed the feasibility of improving the energy bandgap, carrier separation, and light absorption range by C-dots doping, which also lays a favorable foundation for the synthesis of high-efficiency photocatalytic materials loaded with carbon quantum dots. However, limited research has focused on the extensive modification of FA to tap its photocatalytic potential through C-dots loading, especially phosphorized C-dots loading. However, for an ideal photocatalytic material, in addition to the photocatalytic performance, the economic cost and the impact on the environment need to be considered. In order to effectively reduce costs, good recyclability is required. In addition, in view of the potential risks of industrial waste FA, the impact of secondary production materials based on FA on the environment should also be considered. Thus, the prepared material needs to have good stability, prevent pollution to the environment. Therefore, the recovery and recycling of materials are of vital importance [28].

In this study, a three-layer composite was constructed by introducing phosphorized C-dots into the FA surface through energy level regulation. The photogenerated carriers of the C-dots were introduced into the conduction band (CB) of the FA, which improved its photoelectric separation efficiency and optical properties. The FA ball with C-dots was then fixed on the flake carbon layer, which inhibited the rapid recombination of carriers, thereby further improving the photoelectric separation efficiency and stability of the material. The photocatalytic degradation ability of the composite to common Rhodamine B (Rh B) dye was tested, and the effects of the amount of phosphoric acid on the photocatalytic performance were compared. The resulting material exhibited the characteristics of easy molding, easy recycling, and sufficient light absorption, thereby making it highly conducive to various engineering applications.

FA substrate (−125 μm, +150 μm) was supplied by Guizhou Renyizhi Technology Co. Ltd. (China). Sucrose, phosphoric acid, and Rh B were purchased from Chengdu Cologne Chemicals Co. Ltd. (China). All chemicals, reagents, and solvents were of analytical grade and used as received without further purification. Ultrapure water (18.2 MΩ cm) was used in all experiments.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of P–C-dots@FA/P–C, P–C-dots@FA, and P–C
ce:section.ce:para: The composites were synthesized by a one-step microwave method [29]. First, 5 g sucrose was added in a 250 mL beaker. Subsequently, 0.5, 1.0, and 2.0 mL phosphoric acid were added, respectively, to 10 mL ultrapure water for dilution. After the complete dissolution of sucrose acid, 2 g FA was added for the microwave treatment at 700 W for 4 min. After natural cooling to room temperature (298 ± 5 K), the composites were ground to 300 mesh size, washed three times with distilled water, and centrifuged at 4000 rpm for 10 min. According to the mass fraction of phosphoric acid (5, 10, and 18 wt%), the as-prepared materials were labeled as P–C-dots@FA/5P–C, P–C-dots@FA/10P–C, and P–C-dots@FA/18P–C, respectively. To obtain the material with C-dots on the FA surface without the formation of a carbon layer, the microwave synthesis of the P–C-dots@FA/18P–C at 700 W was adjusted to 90 s; the resulting samples were labeled P–C-dots@FA. The FA ratio in P–C-dots@FA/18P–C was adjusted to zero to obtain 18P–C.

X-ray diffraction analysis was performed on an RIGAKU X-ray powder diffractometer Ultima IV (Japan) at a range of 5°–90°, excitation voltage of 40 kV, excitation current of 40 mA, and copper target with a step size of 0.02°. The morphology of the samples was characterized using scanning electron microscopy (Thermo Scientific Apreo 2c, USA) and transmission electron microscopy (TEM, Fei Talos f200x, USA). The chemical composition and valence band (VB) were analyzed using X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS, Thermo Fisher Escalab Xi + spectrometer, USA). The electron paramagnetic resonance (EPR) signals were recorded on a Bruker ELEXSYS II E 500 nanospectrometer (USA) at room temperature (298 K). The Brunauer–Emmett–Teller (BET) specific surface area of the sample was determined by nitrogen adsorption at 77.35 K after vacuum degassing at 473.15 K. The surface groups were analyzed using Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectroscopy (Thermo Fisher Nicolet iS10, USA). The ultraviolet–visible (UV–vis) diffuse reflectance spectra were obtained using a UV–vis spectrometer (Shimadzu UV3600 plus, Japan). The photoluminescence (PL) spectra were recorded by a fluorescence spectrometer (Hitachi f-7100, Japan) at an excitation wavelength of 368 nm and room temperature (298 K). The elemental content was determined by inductively coupled plasma mass spectrometry (Agilent 7800, USA). Electrochemical impedance spectroscopy was carried out on a Chi660e Electrochemical Workstation (Chenhua, China) at an AC voltage of 10 mV, amplitude of 0.5 m Na2SO4, frequency range of 0.1 Hz–100 kHz, and initial potential of −1.0 V. For the photocurrent (PC) measurement, a 300 W xenon lamp was used with a cut-off wavelength of 400 nm as the light source and potassium ferricyanide solution (2.5 mmol/L) as the electrolyte. A standard three-electrode battery was used with indium–tin–oxide-coated conductive glass electrode, platinum electrode, and standard Ag/AgCl as the working, auxiliary, and reference electrodes, respectively. A powder sample of 10 mg was weighted and dispersed in 1 mL ultrapure aqueous solution. Subsequently, 50 μL Nafion solution was added and ultrasonicated for 30 min to form a uniform suspension. A 150 μL suspension was dropped on indium–tin–oxide glass and dried at room temperature for the photoelectric test.

The photocatalytic activity of the as-prepared samples was evaluated by the decolorization of Rh B using a xenon lamp (DY300G, Guangzhou Xing Chuang Electronics Co. Ltd., China) with a 400-nm cut-off filter as the light source. Briefly, 25 mg photocatalyst was dispersed in 25 mL Rh B aqueous solution (50 mg/L). The solution was continuously stirred in a dark environment for 80 min to ensure the adsorption–desorption equilibrium between the photocatalyst and Rh B. After equilibration, the solution was directly moved to the xenon lamp (300 W) light reaction chamber for the photocatalytic degradation experiment. A reaction sample was obtained every 15 min for the centrifugation to remove the solid catalyst and detect the concentration change of Rh B. The absorption characteristic peak at a wavelength of 554 nm was detected by a UV–vis spectrophotometer (UV–9000S, Shanghai Metash Instruments Co., Ltd., China), which indicates the Rh B concentration in the aqueous solution [30].

According to the composition of the P–C-dots@FA/18P–C composites analyzed using energy-dispersive X-ray spectroscopy, as shown in Fig. 1(e)), the characteristic elements of FA (Fe, Ca, Si, and Al) were distributed on the substrate carbon. Based on the dense and extensive carbon distribution on the FA surface, and analysis of Fe and Al with high content detected in the reaction solution (Table S1), some elements in the FA reacted with the phosphoric acid in the reaction solution under microwave, thereby generating hydrogen or other gases. This resulted in the fluffy structure of the P–C-dots@FA/18P–C composite and formation of the carbon layer with the metal dissolved in the solution (Table S1). Furthermore, from the high-magnification TEM images (Fig. 2(c)), the C-dots structure had no obvious lattice at the current scale and non-uniform distribution between the layers. In addition, Figs. 2(a and b) show the structure of P–C-dots@FA/18P–C, which was similar to that of P–C-dots@FA, suggesting the wide distribution of the uneven C-dots. TEM energy-dispersive X-ray spectroscopy further demonstrated the high carbon content on the FA surface, and high P, Si, and O contents. Meanwhile, few Fe and Al metal elements were noted, which further confirmed the loss of the metal elements in the FA and their transfer to the carbon layer during the microwave process.

Fig. 3 (a) presents the X-ray diffraction patterns of 18P–C, P–C-dots@FA/18P–C, P–C-dots@FA, and FA. Typically, 18P–C has a strong (002) peak at 2θ = 26° [31] and weak (101) peak at 2θ of approximately 44°, which is also observed for P–C-dots@FA/18P–C and P–C-dots@FA. Its wide peak indicates its poor crystallinity, which may be caused by the uneven heating process of the reaction liquid by microwave. In addition, after the addition of FA, the peak at 2θ = 26° shifted, which may be due to the appearance of FA particles, so that the carbon layer formed by the solution is no longer a completely free three-dimensional growth, which is hindered by FA particles. Therefore, the molding causes the shift of the carbon peak. The (002) crystal plane was attributed to the layer stacking of unsaturated carbon [32]. FA with the main components of Al2O3, SiO2, Fe2O3, and CaO exhibited a similar structure to mineral mullite with the characteristic peaks at 2θ = 16°, 26°, and 40° [PDF #79–1276]. Meanwhile, the peaks in P–C-dots@FA/18P–C and P–C-dots@FA were relatively weak owing to the destruction of the original crystal structure of FA when it reacted with phosphoric acid during the microwave reaction [33].

Fig. 3(b) shows the FTIR spectra of 18P–C, P–C-dots@FA/18P–C, P–C-dots@FA, and FA. The broad band at 3500–3100 cm−1 was assigned to the OH stretching vibration mode of water. The stretching and bending vibration modes of –CH were noted at 2920 and 990 cm−1, respectively, for the materials with carbon (18P–C, P–C-dots@FA/18P–C, and P–C-dots@FA) [34]. In addition, the peaks at approximately 1630 cm−1 are attributed to C=C of 18P–C, P–C-dots@FA, and P–C-dots@FA/18P–C. From the FTIR spectra, strong peaks were noted for SiO2 in FA, P–C-dots@FA/18P–C, and P–C-dots@FA. The materials with FA base exhibited Si–O–Si stretching and bending vibrations at 1090 and 799 cm−1, respectively.

The elemental compositions of FA, P–C-dots@FA, P–C-dots@FA/18P–C, and 18P–C were analyzed using XPS. In the XPS spectra (Fig. 4 (a)), weak Fe peaks and a strong Si peak were noted. At 133 eV, a P peak was noted, except for FA and P–C-dots@FA, implying their P contents of less than 5%. The characteristic peaks of P at 134.3 eV, which is attributed to [PO3−], was observed in 18P–C. In contrast, for P–C-dots@FA/18P–C, the peaks of P–C and P–O were noted at 131.6 and 132.4 eV [35–37], respectively, indicating that the FA addition combined phosphorus with carbon because carbon alone is easier to combine with oxygen in phosphoric acid. Similarly, according to the Al element analysis (Fig. 4(c)) of the four materials, the Al–O peak was located at 74.5 eV. This Al peak significantly weakened after microwave reaction, which indirectly explains the chemical reaction process between FA and phosphoric acid. Fig. S2(a) shows the carbon peak diagram of the five materials. FA exhibits a relatively simple composition, mainly with C–C and C=O peaks of 284.7 and 287.3 eV, respectively [38,39]. The carbon layer of P–C-dots@FA on the FA surface exhibited the structure of the C=C bond [40], which was also observed in 18P–C and P–C-dots@FA/18P–C, suggesting that electron-rich phosphorus enhanced the conjugation between carbon, which is conducive to carrier transfer. In addition, electron-rich groups, such as C–O bond, were observed in P–C-dots@FA/18P–C, indicating the diversity of its carbon elements.

Visible light catalysis of semiconductors is generally considered as a process: after the material absorbs visible light, electrons located in the VB are excited into the CB. Some photogenerated electrons are separated from holes, electrons and holes are transferred to the material, surface respectively, and utilized by the groups on the surface of the material. The other part rapidly recombines to the ground state in the form of light or heat (this process is not conducive to the photocatalytic performance). Therefore, the photocatalytic performance of materials is mainly controlled by four factors: carrier generation rate, carrier recombination rate, carrier transfer rate to material surface, and the surface reaction rate. Therefore, the properties of carriers have a great influence on the photocatalytic reaction, we first studied the characteristics of material carriers.

In order to realize the rapid generation of carriers, in addition to the need to make it easier for VB electrons to be excited to the CB, it is also necessary to rapidly separate electrons and holes. Considering the bondage between photogenerated electrons and holes, in order to overcome this constraint, we need the support of external forces. It can be realized by adjusting the internal electric field of the material; however, this method has limitations for amorphous materials, therefore, it can be realized by constructing an external electric field, and constructing heterojunction is an ideal method. So, the photoelectrochemical measurements were conducted to analyze the excitation and transfer of the photogenerated carriers as shown in Fig. 5 . When the light source was turned on, the PC signal in the sample increased and then decreased rapidly to zero after a slow decline (Fig. 5(a)). The materials had anodic PC, suggesting that the photogenerated holes were the major carriers in FA, 18P–C, P–C-dots@FA, and P–C-dots@FA/18P–C. The intensity of the PC signals was as follows: P–C-dots@FA/18P–C > P–C-dots@FA > 18P–C > FA. Thus, the slow carrier separation rate in the FA limited its photocatalytic performance, whereas the surface C-dots loaded on P–C-dots@FA effectively increased the carrier separation efficiency. The heterojunction formed by P-C-dots and FA accelerates the electron hole separation and effectively improves the photoelectric separation efficiency of the material. Further, the carbon layer 18P–C of P–C-dots@FA/18P–C promoted the carrier separation, indicating that its structure effectively addressed the problem of low photoelectric separation efficiency of FA.

Rapid photoelectric separation is advantageous to form more carriers; however, these carriers need to be transferred to the surface of the material to be effectively utilized. It is necessary to reduce the resistance between the transfer of carriers from the inside to the material surface. It is generally believed that the carrier transport speed can be effectively improved by reducing the thickness of the material [41], so that carriers are transferred to the material surface more quickly; therefore, the thickness of the carbon layer may have a great influence on the carrier transfer of the material. Therefore, the photoinduced charge transfer rates in FA, 18P–C, P–C-dots@FA, and P–C-dots@FA/18P–C were studied using electrochemical impedance spectroscopy, as shown in Fig. 5(b). The smallest semicircle radius, which is slightly smaller than that of the base material FA, was formed in P–C-dots@FA. Meanwhile, the largest radius was noted in 18P–C. A smaller radius means a smaller resistance, which is more conducive to the transfer of carriers from the inside of the material to the surface of the material. Compared with composite materials, this implies that FA itself has an excellent performance on interfacial charge transfer, whereby the carbon on the FA surface increased its charge transfer rate. Meanwhile, the carbon substrate 18P–C exhibited a poor conductivity. This may be due to the weak order of stacked carbon layers, which makes the photogenerated carriers generated by 18P-C more likely to collide, thus significantly reducing the carrier transfer rate. This implies that the photogenerated carriers involved in the surface reaction may be more from P–C-dots or FA.

The recombination of photo generated carriers means that photo generated electrons and holes annihilate in the form of light or heat under the action of Coulomb force. In other words, the available carriers on the material surface are greatly reduced, so the recombination of carriers must be effectively suppressed for photocatalytic materials [42]. Among many methods for suppressing carrier recombination, constructing heterojunction is a simple, fast, and effective method. Therefore, we designed to construct type II heterojunction between carbon-dots and FA, thereby reducing the composite rate of the material. Our conjecture was verified by PL spectroscopy, as shown in Fig. 5(c). The light response signal at approximately 460 nm was observed with the fluorescence intensity of FA > P–C-dots@FA > P–C-dots@FA/18P–C > 18P–C. The strong fluorescence response of FA was attributed to the rapid recombination of its photogenerated carriers, which greatly limited the photocatalytic performance of FA. Meanwhile, the P–C-dots@FA formed by the carbon deposited on the FA surface effectively inhibited the recombination of the carriers. This confirms our conjecture, the formation of heterojunction between P–C-dots and FA effectively reduces the recombination rate of photogenerated carriers. Further, as a supporter, 18P–C significantly quenched the fluorescence signal and increased the utilization efficiency of the carriers. Thus, the multilevel structure of P–C-dots@FA/18P–C effectively solved the problem of rapid recombination of the FA photogenerated carriers.

For a photocatalytic oxidation reaction, its energy band structure directly affects the photocatalytic performance of the material. The band gap of the material directly affects the generation and recombination of carriers, and a narrow band gap is conducive to the absorption of light and the generation of photogenerated electrons. However, the narrow band gap may also cause the rapid recombination of carriers, so it is of great significance to properly control the band gap of materials. Therefore, the energy band structure of materials should be explored. Fig. 6 (a) shows the UV–vis absorption spectra of P–C-dots@FA/18P–C and P–C-dots@FA. FA exhibited weak visible light absorption at 400–600 nm. Meanwhile, P–C-dots@FA showed a stronger absorption in the visible light range than FA. Compared with18P-C, P–C-dots@FA and P–C-dots@FA/18P–C exhibited a wider and stronger absorption of visible light. The absorbance of P–C-dots@FA/18P–C in the near-infrared region is greatly improved, the utilization of visible light is enhanced, and the probability of carrier generation is effectively increased. Equation (1) was used to estimate the forbidden bandwidth of the material [43]:
(1)
ahv = A
(
hv −
E g
)
n / 2
,
where the corresponding forbidden bandwidths of FA, 18P–C, P–C-dots@FA/18P–C, and P–C-dots@FA are 2.40, 1.34, 1.67, and 1.81 eV, as shown in Fig. 6(b).

Further, FA, 18P–C, P–C-dots@FA/18P–C, and P–C-dots@FA were characterized using Mott–Schottky, as shown in Fig. 7 . The tangent slope of the Mott–Schottky diagram and X-axis was positive, indicating that the resulting materials were n-type semiconductors. The intersection point represents the flat-band potential of the material, which were −0.29, −0.71, −0.26, and −0.56 eV for FA, 18P–C, P–C-dots@FA, and P–C-dots@FA/18P–C, respectively. The corresponding CB bandgap can be estimated as −0.19, −0.61, −0.16, and −0.46 eV for FA, 18P-C, P–C-dots@FA, and P–C-dots@FA/18P–C, respectively. Combined with the corresponding band gap Eg of 2.40, 1.34, 1.81, and 1.67 eV, the VBs of FA, 18P–C, P–C-dots@FA, and P–C-dots@FA/18P–C were 2.21, 0.73, 1.21, and 1.65 eV, respectively.

Considering the influence of the position of band edges on the formation of free radicals, the energy band structure of the prepared materials was summarized. As shown in Fig. 8 , the potential of O2 conversion to ·O2− in typical water is 0.33 eV, except for FA and P–C-dots@FA, the CB positions of the materials in the prepared 4 are all higher than −0.33 eV, which means that P–C-dots@FA/18P–C can generate ·O2− by reducing the adsorbed O2 through the CB, and FA itself is difficult to achieve this process. While the potentials of OH− and H2O in water to ·OH are 1.99 and 2.37 eV, respectively. However, only the potential of FA VB is slightly higher than 1.99 eV, but still lower than 2.37 eV. This potential is not conducive to the production of ·OH directly through the VB. The same VB potential also implies the direct oxidation ability of h+. The VB potential of composite P–C-dots@FA/18P–C is 1.21 eV, and its h+ oxidation energy is relatively weak. The band structure of P–C-dots@FA/18P–C suggests that it may participate in the degradation process mainly through O2.

From the adsorption analysis of the materials, the adsorption curves tended to stabilize after 80 min (Fig. 9 (a)). The Rh B adsorption efficiencies of FA and P–FA were only 2.50% and 3.24%, respectively, compared to the carbonaceous material. The poor adsorption ability against the non-radical reaction can restrict the photodegradation of FA. The adsorption of P–C-dots@FA, 18P–C, and P–C-dots@FA/18P–C after 90 min were 8.29%, 32.37%, and 34.60%, respectively. From the comparative study of P–C-dots@FA and P–C-dots@FA/x P–C (where x is the ratio of the phosphoric acid), the carbon layer greatly increased the Rh B adsorption of the material, which is significantly better than the C-dots on the FA surface. Combined with the BET characterization, the results indicated that the adsorption can be attributed to the chemical bond. Further, the Rh B adsorption efficiency of P–C-dots@FA/x P–C increased with the increase in the amount of phosphoric acid. Therefore, the carbon layer improved the adsorption performance of the material, which can be beneficial to the degradation of the non-radical reaction.

The photocatalytic activity of the materials was investigated, as shown in Fig. 9(b). The degradation of FA, P–FA, 18P–C, P–C-dots@FA, P–C-dots@FA/5P–C, P–C-dots@FA/10P–C, and P–C-dots@FA/18P–C were 0.39%, 11.24%, 56.9%, 21.25%, 39.66%, 50.01%, and 92.96%, respectively, under visible light irradiation for 150 min after the adsorption equilibrium under a dark environment. Thus, FA can degrade a minimal amount of Rh B. However, P–FA, which was obtained by phosphorus doping on FA, exhibited their slight ability to degrade Rh B. The photodegradation efficiency for Rh B increased with the increase in the amount of phosphoric acid. The photocatalytic degradation ability of P–C-dots@FA/x P–C improved with the increase in the phosphoric acid addition. The comparison of FA, P–C-dots@FA, and P–C-dots@FA/18P–C showed that the C-dots on the FA surface increased the photodegradation ability of the material, and the carbon layer loaded with FA greatly improved the performance of the photocatalysts. The reaction of the photocatalytic degradation of Rh B can be described by the pseudo first-order kinetic equation:
(2)
− ln
(
C t
C 0
)
=
k
a p p
t
,
where C0 and Ct (mg/L) are the reactant concentrations at 0 and t, respectively, and kapp is the constant apparent reaction rate [44]. P–C-dots@FA/18P–C exhibited a larger value for the reaction rate constant (kapp) than FA, 18P–C, and P–C-dots@FA, which indicates a more effective photocatalytic activity (Fig. 9(c)). Thus, P–C-dots@FA/18P–C demonstrated a good photocatalytic performance.

The reproducibility of the material was studied. Five control groups were set up, corresponding to five different microwave reactors (the power, microwave time, and other reaction conditions were the same). Ten samples were prepared in each group, a total of 50 samples, and their performances were tested. Among them, the photocatalytic performance is shown in Fig. 10 (a). The colored boxes in the figure indicate the differences within each group. Most of the data are relatively concentrated in one area. Through calculation, we can also get that the standard deviation between groups (group1–group5) is 0.86%, 1.21%, 1.37%, 1.39%, and 1.56%, respectively. This indicates that the photocatalytic performance of the materials prepared in the same reactor is relatively stable without too much fluctuation. In addition, it can be observed that most of the data are concentrated between the gray lines, and the standard deviation between 50 samples is calculated to be 2.00%. Although there are some differences between the groups, this difference is acceptable. All these show that the experimental method has a good stability; especially, the material properties between the same group remain stable. Further, in order to understand the value of material recycling, 1 sample (5 samples in total) was selected from each group for 5 cycle experiments. As shown in Fig. 10(b), a small error bar proved that the recovery process was relatively stable. After 5 cycle experiments, the average photocatalytic degradation rate of the material decreased from 79.8% to 76.9%, and the overall loss was small, which proved that the material had certain recyclability. In addition, we also recorded the weight of materials in each recycling process, such as shown in Table S3; the total mass loss of the materials in the five experimental groups is less than 8% after five cycles. However, considering the accuracy of the analytical balance, the actual loss may be smaller.

For a photocatalytic degradation reaction, it usually includes homogeneous and heterogeneous parts. The homogeneous reaction is mainly dominated by free radicals, while the heterogeneous reaction participates in the reaction by the direct oxidation of h+ or the formation of free radicals by h+. Therefore, it is particularly important to understand these two processes. First of all, the heterogeneous reaction can be simply summarized as five processes: adsorption, dissociation, diffusion, bonding, desorption. Adsorption, as the first process, has attracted much attention. It is generally believed that the adsorption capacity of the material to the target should not be too strong or too weak. Therefore, we studied the adsorption of Rh B on the materials. A scatter plot of their adsorption rate photocatalytic degradation rate was drawn based on 50 parallel experiments of P–C-dots@FA/18P–C samples. As shown in Fig. 11 , the Rh B adsorption rate and its photocatalytic Rh B degradation rate among 50 parallel samples were linearly fitted, and the correlation coefficient R2 = 0.79 was obtained. This indicates that within a certain adsorption range, the photocatalytic performance of P–C-dots@FA/18P–C has a significant relationship with its Rh B adsorption rate. With the increase of the adsorption capacity of Rh B, the degradation rate of the material increases. This implies that the good photocatalysis of the prepared carbon layer to the material may be partly attributed to its good adsorption to Rh B.

The radical species play a crucial role in the photocatalytic degradation of the Rh B aqueous solution. To investigate the primary active species, ammonium oxalate, p-benzoquinone, and isopropanol were used as masking agents for h+, ·O2− , and ·OH, respectively [45–47]. When p-benzoquinone was added, the Rh B removal was inhibited from 93.75% to 27.80% (Fig. S3(a)). The addition of ammonium oxalate and isopropanol exhibited slight inhibition on the photocatalytic performance, indicating the minimal effect of h+ and ·OH on the Rh B degradation. The masking experiment shows that the effect of h+ is relatively weak during the degradation reaction, while the strong inhibition of ·O2− on the reaction means that ·O2− may be the free radical that plays a major role in the degradation reaction. Considering that masking ·OH also has a certain inhibitory effect on the reaction, while the effect of h+ is weak, it can be inferred that part of ·O2− may be converted into ·OH in the solution.

EPR was used to further investigate the effect of ·O2− and ·OH radicals on the removal of organic pollutants. As the scavenger, 5,5-dimethyl-1-pyrroline N-oxide (DMPO) was dissolved in a methanol and water solution to detect the presence of ·O2− and ·OH during the reactions, respectively. As shown in Fig. 12 , no EPR signals were observed in the sample under a dark environment, whereas the signals were clearly detected under visible light irradiation for 20 min. This indicates the importance of visible light for the generation of ·O2 − and ·OH. Six characteristic peaks of DMPO-O2 − (Fig. 12(a)) were detected for P–C-dots@FA/18P–C under visible light irradiation of 20 min. This is attributed to the single electron reduction of O2 to ·O2 − and thermomechanical feasibility of the CB (−0.46 eV) of P–C-dots@FA/18P–C (−0.33 eV for O2/·O2 −) [48]. Fig. 12(b) illustrates that the weak DMPO-·OH peaks for P–C/FA/18P–C tested after irradiation for 20 min. This is attributed to the visible light that can only excite P–C-dots@FA/18P–C, and the VB hole (1.21 eV) from P–C-dots@FA/18P–C that cannot directly oxidize OH− or H2O to the ·OH radical (1.99 eV for OH−/·OH and 2.37 eV for H2O/·OH) [49]. The ·OH radicals should be generated through the ·O2 −→H2O2→·OH route. Thus, h+, ·O2 −, and ·OH were generated during the photodegradation reaction, and h+ and ·O2− played a predominant role toward the degradation of organic pollutants.

Therefore, we proposed a possible Rh B degradation mechanism of the synthesized materials. After being excited, the electrons on the VB of the lamellar carbon and C-dots quickly transferred to their respective CBs. Owing to the contact with the FA interface, the energy band was bent, and the carriers formed can easily flow into the CB of FA. The oxygen in water was adsorbed on the FA surface and reduced to superoxide radicals by the CB electrons. Moreover, owing to the interface effect of the carbon layer and C-dots with FA, the electrons of the FA can be easily excited to the VB of the C-dots or layered carbon with a relatively lower energy, thereby further improving the oxidation ability of the holes. Therefore, the degradation of Rh B by the synthesized materials was dominated by superoxide radicals with a certain role attributed to the holes and hydroxyl radicals.

In this work, FA with poor photocatalytic performance was modified by a one-step microwave synthesis and two-step photogenerated carrier transfer method to improve its original photocatalytic activity. The resulting materials were successfully applied to the Rh B degradation in water. Under microwave, C-dots were first formed on the FA surface, which increased the effective charge separation and transfer, and reduced the bandgap width. However, the photocatalysis performance was limited owing to the rapid recombination of the photogenerated carriers. Furthermore, with an increase in the microwave time, the mixed solution of sucrose and phosphoric acid was carbonized gradually, and a carbon layer was formed on the FA surface, which significantly inhibited the recombination of the photogenerated carriers and improved the photocatalytic performance. The porous carbon layer was conducive to the adsorption of Rh B.

The resulting composite structure effectively improved the photocatalytic activity of FA. The degradation of Rh B in water was mainly attributed to the ·O2− radicals generated by the material under the irradiation of natural light. This strategy effectively utilized the high efficiency of the photoinduced charge carriers in the obsolete FA. The facile microwave modification achieved a good photocatalytic performance and application value of the resulting photocatalytic materials. The 50 parallel experiments of the material have proved that the material has good reproducibility. The sample difference between the same group is small and the sample difference between different groups is small, which is conducive to production control. The five cycle experiments of five groups of samples proved that the prepared materials were easy to recycle and reuse, and the mass loss and degradation effect loss were small each time. This work offers new thinking on the design and manufacture of new photocatalysts with multi-layer linkage action. As well as provides an effective new way to the efficient transfer of carriers and the suppression of their recombination.

Siyu He: Formal analysis, Investigation, Writing - original draft, Writing - review & editing. Lingzhi Luo: Investigation, Formal analysis, Data curation, Writing - original draft. Tingting Jiang: Investigation, Data curation. Guanlong Wang: Investigation, Data curation. Yaoqi Liu: Data curation, Supervision. Tianbin Fan: Writing - review & editing, Supervision.

Xingyu Zhu: Methodology, Software, Visualization. Xing Wei: Methodology, Software, Visualization. Fujun Wang: Writing - review & editing, Supervision. Jiang Jing: Conceptualization, Writing - review & editing, Supervision, Project administration, Funding acquisition.
